perturbation.original_text: Exclusions from Confidential Information. Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement do not extend to information that is: (a) publicly known at the time of disclosure or subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) discovered or created by the Receiving Party before disclosure by Disclosing Party; (c) learned by the Receiving Party through legitimate means other than from the Disclosing Party or Disclosing Party's representatives; or (d) is disclosed by Receiving Party with Disclosing Party's prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Exclusions from Confidential Information. Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement do not extend to information that is: (a) publicly known at the time of disclosure or subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) discovered or created by the Receiving Party before disclosure by Disclosing Party; (c) learned by the Receiving Party through legitimate means other than from the Disclosing Party or Disclosing Party's representatives.
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause (d) 'is disclosed by Receiving Party with Disclosing Party's prior written approval', we introduce a contradiction. The original clause allowed the Receiving Party to disclose information with written approval, while now, ANY disclosure would be a violation. This omission impacts the Receiving Party's flexibility and creates a stricter, less nuanced agreement than originally intended, leading to a contradictory position. Clause 4 is contradicticed by not having the possibility of disclosing the infomration with prior written consent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Receiving Party. Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement. Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information. Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, documents, graphics, electronic files, notes, and other written, printed, or electronic copies, or other tangible materials or digital files in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits clause (d) from the original text, which allowed disclosure of confidential information with the Disclosing Party's prior written approval. The contradicted text states, 'Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others...any Confidential Information.' The original clause created an exception to the non-disclosure obligation. By removing the exception, the changed text introduces a strict prohibition on disclosure, thereby creating a direct contradiction with the contradicted section that contemplates and allows for disclosure with prior written approval.

perturbation.original_text: Pre-Existing Relationships. In the event that any third parties already have a pre-existing working relationship or have previously made contact with any parties to this Agreement via other means, and such third parties were not introduced by any parties to this Agreement, such third party contacts and/or sources are not subject to the restrictions, terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Pre-Existing Relationships. In the event that any third parties already have a pre-existing working relationship or have previously made contact with any parties to this Agreement via other means, such third party contacts and/or sources are not subject to the restrictions, terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'and such third parties were not introduced by any parties to this Agreement' creates an in-text contradiction. Originally, pre-existing relationships were exempt only if the third party was NOT introduced by the parties in the agreement. Now, ALL pre-existing relationships are exempt, regardless of who made the introduction. This contradicts the spirit of non-circumvention and allows parties to circumvent the agreement through pre-existing relationships, even if those relationships were facilitated by the disclosing party. This impacts Section 6 'Non-Circumvention'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Circumvention. The Receiving Party expressly agrees not to circumvent, avoid, bypass or obviate the Disclosing Party in any manner, directly or indirectly, to avoid payment of fees, royalties, or any other obligation in any way involving any of the parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, or any transaction contemplated hereunder or other business arrangement arising out of or connected in any way to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the condition that pre-existing relationships are only exempt from the agreement's restrictions if the third party was NOT introduced by the parties to the agreement. This creates a direct conflict with the 'Non-Circumvention' clause, as the receiving party could now circumvent the agreement through pre-existing relationships, even if those relationships were originally facilitated by the disclosing party. This contradicts the original intent and directly undermines the non-circumvention obligation. As the definition states, this omission causes an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall restrict access to Confidential Information to only those employees directly involved in the evaluation and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required" to "only those employees directly involved in the evaluation", the Receiving Party's responsibility is reduced. This omission creates a contradiction because other clauses still assume a broader restriction scope. For instance, if a contractor was previously allowed access, the agreement now doesn't permit it, causing confusion. This potentially contradicts the broad definition of Confidential Information (modified in perturbation 1) and creates a loophole for unauthorized disclosure by individuals other than 'directly involved employees', going against the 'Non-Circumvention' purpose of the agreement defined in section 6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Non-Circumvention. The Receiving Party expressly agrees not to circumvent, avoid, bypass or obviate the Disclosing Party in any manner, directly or indirectly, to avoid payment of fees, royalties, or any other obligation in any way involving any of the parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, or any transaction contemplated hereunder or other business arrangement arising out of or connected in any way to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed access to confidential information to "any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required." The changed text restricts access to "only those employees directly involved in the evaluation." This creates a direct contradiction with the Non-Circumvention clause, which requires preventing the circumvention of the Disclosing Party by "any of the parties introduced and Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement." If a contractor or agent previously had access under the original agreement, but is now excluded, the agreement no longer effectively prevents them from circumventing the Disclosing Party, thus directly contradicting the Non-Circumvention clause. The omission creates an inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: Time Periods. The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret' implies confidentiality is perpetual unless explicitly released by the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the implied possibility of information becoming publicly available and losing trade secret status, as outlined in section 2, creating an in-text contradiction where the receiving party could argue a conflict due to the information no longer being confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exclusions from Confidential Information. Receiving Party's obligations under this Agreement do not extend to information that is: (a) publicly known at the time of disclosure or subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of the Receiving Party; (b) discovered or created by the Receiving Party before disclosure by Disclosing Party; (c) learned by the Receiving Party through legitimate means other than from the Disclosing Party or Disclosing Party's representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the Receiving Party's duty of confidentiality to end either when the information was no longer a trade secret or upon written notice. The changed text removes the condition that confidentiality ends when the information is no longer a trade secret. This creates a contradiction with the 'Exclusions from Confidential Information' section, specifically part (a), which states that the Receiving Party has no obligation to treat information as confidential if it becomes publicly known through no fault of their own. Because the changed text suggests perpetual confidentiality unless a waiver is granted, it contradicts the scenario where information independently becomes public knowledge and no longer qualifies as a trade secret, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a business agreement, contract, or other business arrangement is entered into, each Party agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation, all Confidential Information in any form upon conclusion of the evaluation of entering into the transaction or other business arrangement or upon the specific request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation, all Confidential Information in any form upon the specific request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "Unless a business agreement, contract, or other business arrangement is entered into, each Party agrees to return or destroy, with written confirmation, all Confidential Information in any form upon conclusion of the evaluation of entering into the transaction or other business arrangement or", the modified text now mandates the return/destruction of confidential information ONLY upon request. This creates an in-text contradiction to the general purpose of the agreement, defined on the first section, which aims at avoiding the disclosure of confidential information. Thus not requiring the destruction after concluding the business agreement, now makes possible for the Receiving party to leverage the confidential information after the deal, damaging the Desclosing party. Furthermore, it contradicticed the Obliations of the Receiving Party stated in Section 4, where a continuous duty to the information is established.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations of Receiving Party. Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Confidential Information to any employees, contractors, agents, and any other third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign non-disclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement. Receiving Party shall not, without prior written approval of Disclosing Party, use for Receiving Party's own benefit, publish, copy, transmit, use, or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of Disclosing Party, any Confidential Information. Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, documents, graphics, electronic files, notes, and other written, printed, or electronic copies, or other tangible materials or digital files in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the return/destruction of confidential information upon conclusion of the evaluation OR upon request of the disclosing party. The changed text eliminates the requirement for return/destruction upon conclusion of the evaluation, leaving only the 'upon request' condition. This directly contradicts the 'Obligations of Receiving Party' section, which establishes a broader duty to maintain confidentiality and restrict use, and does not limit the obligation to only cases when the disclosing party makes a specific request. The removal of the destruction requirement upon conclusion of the business agreement allows the Receiving party to potentially leverage confidential information even after the deal, directly conflicting with the receiving party's continuous duty to the information and the exclusive benefit of the disclosing party, and potentially harming the Disclosing party, as it is explained in the Perturbation Explanation.
